specification.topic-statement: The cursor position calculator determines the visual screen row and column of a cursor within a UTF-8 text buffer, accounting for line wrapping and ANSI escape sequences.

scope.in-scope: Translating a byte offset within a UTF-8 text buffer into a zero-based (row, column) screen coordinate pair, given a terminal width.
scope.boundary: Each non-newline, non-escape byte sequence at the current position is decoded to determine its Unicode codepoint. The byte count consumed and the resulting codepoint are used to advance the column and byte position.

When the current byte begins an ANSI CSI (Control Sequence Introducer) escape sequence, the byte span of the complete sequence is provided so the cursor can skip past it without counting display columns.

The computed (row, column) pair is written to a caller-provided output location. Callers convert the zero-based coordinates to one-based terminal coordinates before issuing ANSI cursor-positioning commands.

field.name: text buffer
field.note: must be non-null; length may be zero

field.name: cursor byte offset
field.note: byte position to resolve; may exceed text length

behavior.initialization: Both the current row and current column begin at zero. The scan position begins at the start of the buffer.
behavior.scanning:
The buffer is scanned from the start toward the cursor byte offset. Each byte is classified in order of priority: newline, ANSI escape, then UTF-8 character.
If the current byte is a newline character (0x0A), the row counter increments by one and the column resets to zero. The scan position advances by one byte. No other processing is done for this byte.
Only 0x0A is treated as a line terminator. A carriage return (0x0D) is not recognized as a newline and is instead processed as a regular character through UTF-8 decoding.
If the current position begins a valid ANSI CSI escape sequence, the entire sequence is skipped without changing the row or column. A valid CSI sequence begins with ESC followed by '[', then zero or more parameter bytes (0x30–0x3F), zero or more intermediate bytes (0x20–0x2F), and a final byte (0x40–0x7E). If the sequence is incomplete before the end of the buffer, it is not recognized as a valid CSI escape.
An unrecognized or incomplete escape at any position causes the byte at that position to be submitted for UTF-8 decoding. The ESC byte (0x1B) is not valid UTF-8, so this results in an error being returned to the caller.
For any byte that is neither a newline nor the start of a recognized ANSI CSI escape, a Unicode codepoint is decoded from the buffer at the current position. If decoding fails (invalid or truncated UTF-8), the calculation terminates immediately and returns an error carrying the byte offset where the invalid sequence was found.
On successful decoding, the display width of the codepoint is determined: - Standard and narrow characters have a width of 1. - Wide characters (CJK ideographs, many emoji) have a width of 2. - Combining characters and most control characters have a width of 0.
Some control codepoints produce a display width of -1. This value is not validated or clamped before being added to the column counter, which can make the column negative and produce undefined arithmetic in subsequent wrap checks.
Before adding the character's width to the column, a wrap check is performed: if the current column plus the character's width exceeds the terminal width, the row increments by one and the column resets to zero. The character's width is then added to the column. The scan position advances by the number of bytes consumed by the UTF-8 sequence.
behavior.post-scan-boundary-wrap:
After the scan completes (when the scan position reaches or passes the cursor byte offset), a final check is applied: if the column equals exactly the terminal width, the row increments by one and the column resets to zero.
This handles the case where the cursor lands precisely at the right edge of a line, placing it at the start of the next line rather than one column past the last visible position.
The exact-boundary wrap is only checked once, after the loop terminates. During the loop, only the strictly-exceeds condition triggers a wrap. A character whose addition brings the column to exactly the terminal width does not wrap inside the loop; the wrap is deferred until after the loop exits.
behavior.output-and-return:
The computed row and column are written to the caller-provided output location. On success, the caller's allocation context is returned as the success value.
On failure (invalid UTF-8 encountered during scanning), an error is returned carrying the error code INVALID_ARG and a message containing the byte offset where decoding failed. The output location is not written on failure.

edge-cases.cursor-offset-exceeds-length: If the cursor byte offset is greater than the text length, the scan loop exits immediately without processing any bytes, and the output is (row=0, col=0). No error is returned.

terminal-width-not-validated.notable: The terminal width is not validated. A value of zero or a negative value causes the exceeds-width condition to be true for every character, wrapping to a new row after each one.
